lesson.menu_title: Present Perfect in Business -- Experience & Results
lesson.menu_desc: Talk about your track record: present perfect for experience and results vs simple past for finished events -- 27 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 15 -- Present Perfect in Business -- Experience & Results
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Maria Claudia Curimbaba | Aula 15 -- Present Perfect in Business

listenings.text: Good morning. I am David Chen, a partner at our industrial group in Houston. Let me give you a short profile of the leader we are about to meet. She has run the family holding for over fifteen years, and her track record speaks for itself. She has built three subsidiaries, and she has closed several major deals with us this year alone. Over the last decade, she has mentored a strong management team. Last year, she led a difficult restructuring, and the results were excellent. She has never missed a quarterly target since she took over. In short, she has the experience, and she has delivered the results. I am confident this will be a productive partnership.

Before the call, I want to think about my own story. I have worked in this business for fifteen years. In that time, I have managed the financial and sales operations, and I have built a team I trust. We have grown the holding every single year. This quarter, we have already closed three new deals, and I am proud of that. There have been hard moments too. In 2019, I led a painful restructuring. It was difficult, but I learned a lot, and it made the company stronger. When I speak with David today, I will share my track record clearly. I will use the present perfect for my experience and my results, and the simple past for the projects I finished in a specific year.

extra_audio.text: First, you list the milestones you have reached in your career. Then, you separate the finished projects, with a year, from your ongoing results. After that, you describe your experience with the present perfect, for example, I have managed three subsidiaries. Next, you describe a specific finished project with the simple past, for example, I led the restructuring in 2019. Finally, you connect your track record to the value you can deliver now.
